revision.text:
{{AfC submission|t||ts=20211107040657|u=CycoMa|ns=118|demo=}}<!-- Important, do not remove this line before article has been created. --> {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = Shinto | image = File:Shrine, Tokyo Daijingu - Chiyoda, Tokyo, Japan - DSC04748.jpg }} '''Tokyo Daijingu''' is a shrine.<ref>{{Cite web|last=Bureau|first=Tokyo Convention & Visitors|title=Tokyo Daijingu Shrine|url=https://www.gotokyo.org/en/spot/42/index.html|access-date=2021-11-07|website=The Official Tokyo Travel Guide, GO TOKYO|language=en}}</ref> it is affectionately called ''O-Ise-sama in Tokyo'' because of the deities enshrined there.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=English - Tokyo Daijingu|url=http://www.tokyodaijingu.or.jp/english/|access-date=2021-11-07|website=www.tokyodaijingu.or.jp|language=ja}}</ref>
== History == It was built in the early [[Meiji (era)|Meiji period]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Hardacre|first=Helen|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=nP7vr5yaaZIC&newbks=0&printsec=frontcover&pg=PA89&dq=Tokyo+Daijingu&hl=en|title=Shinto and the State, 1868-1988|date=1989|publisher=Princeton University Press|isbn=978-0-691-02052-5|language=en}}</ref>{{Rp|89}} It was originally called ''Hibiya Daijingu''.<ref name=":0" />
In 1901 a webbing too place at the shrine offering the first [[Shinto wedding]] for people in urban areas.<ref>{{Cite book|last=de-Gaia|first=Susan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=jt91DwAAQBAJ&pg=RA1-PA286&dq=Tokyo+Daijingu+history&hl=en&newbks=1&newbks_redir=0&source=gb_mobile_search&sa=X&ved=2ahUKEwjk05uUt4b0AhXkgnIEHdlhBAQQ6AF6BAgHEAM|title=Encyclopedia of Women in World Religions: Faith and Culture across History [2 volumes]|date=2018-11-16|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-4408-4850-6|language=en}}</ref>{{Rp|286}}
== Enshrined kami == Deities enshrined here include.<ref name=":0" />
* [[Amaterasu]]
* [[Toyouke-no-Ohkami]]
* [[Ame-no-Minakanushi|Ameno-Minakanushi]]
* [[Takamimusubi]]
* [[Kamimusubi]]
* [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]]
== References == <!-- Inline citations added to your article will automatically display here. See en.wikipedia.org/wiki/WP:REFB for instructions on how to add citations. --> {{reflist}}

It is one of the top five shrines in Tokyo, and is dedicated to love.<ref name=":1" />
== History == It was built in the early [[Meiji (era)|Meiji period]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Hardacre|first=Helen|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=nP7vr5yaaZIC&newbks=0&printsec=frontcover&pg=PA89&dq=Tokyo+Daijingu&hl=en|title=Shinto and the State, 1868-1988|date=1989|publisher=Princeton University Press|isbn=978-0-691-02052-5|language=en}}</ref>{{Rp|89}} It was originally called ''Hibiya Daijingu''.<ref name=":0" />
In 1901 a webbing too place at the shrine offering the first [[Shinto wedding]] for people in urban areas.<ref>{{Cite book|last=de-Gaia|first=Susan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=jt91DwAAQBAJ&pg=RA1-PA286&dq=Tokyo+Daijingu+history&hl=en&newbks=1&newbks_redir=0&source=gb_mobile_search&sa=X&ved=2ahUKEwjk05uUt4b0AhXkgnIEHdlhBAQQ6AF6BAgHEAM|title=Encyclopedia of Women in World Religions: Faith and Culture across History [2 volumes]|date=2018-11-16|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-4408-4850-6|language=en}}</ref>{{Rp|286}}
I’m 1928, it was renamed to ''Iidabashi Daijingu''.
After [[World War II|World War 2]] the place changed its name to Tokyo Daijingu.<ref name=":0" />

{{AfC submission|t||ts=20211107040657|u=CycoMa|ns=118|demo=}}<!-- Important, do not remove this line before article has been created. --> {{Infobox religious building | name = Tokyo Daijingu | religious_affiliation = Shinto | image = File:Shrine, Tokyo Daijingu - Chiyoda, Tokyo, Japan - DSC04748.jpg | website = http://www.tokyodaijingu.or.jp/index.html }} '''Tokyo Daijingu''' is a shrine located in [[Tokyo]].<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|last=Bureau|first=Tokyo Convention & Visitors|title=Tokyo Daijingu Shrine|url=https://www.gotokyo.org/en/spot/42/index.html|access-date=2021-11-07|website=The Official Tokyo Travel Guide, GO TOKYO|language=en}}</ref> The shrine is also called ''O-Ise-sama in Tokyo'' because of the deities enshrined there.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=English - Tokyo Daijingu|url=http://www.tokyodaijingu.or.jp/english/|access-date=2021-11-07|website=www.tokyodaijingu.or.jp|language=ja}}</ref>
It is one of the top five shrines in Tokyo and is dedicated to love.<ref name=":1" />
== History == It was built in the early [[Meiji (era)|Meiji period]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Hardacre|first=Helen|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=nP7vr5yaaZIC&newbks=0&printsec=frontcover&pg=PA89&dq=Tokyo+Daijingu&hl=en|title=Shinto and the State, 1868-1988|date=1989|publisher=Princeton University Press|isbn=978-0-691-02052-5|language=en}}</ref>{{Rp|89}} It was originally called ''Hibiya Daijingu''.<ref name=":0" />
In 1901 a webbing too place at the shrine offering the first [[Shinto wedding]] for people in urban areas.<ref>{{Cite book|last=de-Gaia|first=Susan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=jt91DwAAQBAJ&pg=RA1-PA286&dq=Tokyo+Daijingu+history&hl=en&newbks=1&newbks_redir=0&source=gb_mobile_search&sa=X&ved=2ahUKEwjk05uUt4b0AhXkgnIEHdlhBAQQ6AF6BAgHEAM|title=Encyclopedia of Women in World Religions: Faith and Culture across History [2 volumes]|date=2018-11-16|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-4408-4850-6|language=en}}</ref>{{Rp|286}}
I’m 1928, it was renamed to ''Iidabashi Daijingu''.
After [[World War II|World War 2]] the place changed its name to Tokyo Daijingu.<ref name=":0" />
== Enshrined kami == Deities enshrined here include.<ref name=":0" />
* [[Amaterasu]]
* [[Toyoukebime|Toyouke-no-Ohkami]]
* [[Ame-no-Minakanushi|Ameno-Minakanushi]]
* [[Takamimusubi]]
* [[Kamimusubi]]
* [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]]

In 1901, a webbing too place at the shrine being the first [[Shinto wedding]] for people in urban areas.<ref>{{Cite book|last=de-Gaia|first=Susan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=jt91DwAAQBAJ&pg=RA1-PA286&dq=Tokyo+Daijingu+history&hl=en&newbks=1&newbks_redir=0&source=gb_mobile_search&sa=X&ved=2ahUKEwjk05uUt4b0AhXkgnIEHdlhBAQQ6AF6BAgHEAM|title=Encyclopedia of Women in World Religions: Faith and Culture across History [2 volumes]|date=2018-11-16|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-4408-4850-6|language=en}}</ref>{{Rp|286}}
In 1928, it was renamed to ''Iidabashi Daijingu''.
Then after [[World War II|World War 2]] the place changed its name to Tokyo Daijingu.<ref name=":0" />

== History == The shrine was built in the early [[Meiji (era)|Meiji period]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Hardacre|first=Helen|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=nP7vr5yaaZIC&newbks=0&printsec=frontcover&pg=PA89&dq=Tokyo+Daijingu&hl=en|title=Shinto and the State, 1868-1988|date=1989|publisher=Princeton University Press|isbn=978-0-691-02052-5|language=en}}</ref>{{Rp|89}} so people in Tokyo could worship the deities enshrined at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Grand Shrine of Ise]] from afar. It was originally called ''Hibiya Daijingu''.<ref name=":0" />
In 1901, a webbing too place at the shrine being the first [[Shinto wedding]] for people in urban areas.<ref>{{Cite book|last=de-Gaia|first=Susan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=jt91DwAAQBAJ&pg=RA1-PA286&dq=Tokyo+Daijingu+history&hl=en&newbks=1&newbks_redir=0&source=gb_mobile_search&sa=X&ved=2ahUKEwjk05uUt4b0AhXkgnIEHdlhBAQQ6AF6BAgHEAM|title=Encyclopedia of Women in World Religions: Faith and Culture across History [2 volumes]|date=2018-11-16|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-4408-4850-6|language=en}}</ref>{{Rp|286}}
In 1928, it was renamed to ''Iidabashi Daijingu''. Then after [[World War II|World War 2]] the place changed its name to Tokyo Daijingu.<ref name=":0" />
== Enshrined kami == Deities enshrined here include.<ref name=":0" />
* [[Amaterasu]]
* [[Toyoukebime|Toyouke-no-Ohkami]]
* [[Ame-no-Minakanushi|Ameno-Minakanushi]]
* [[Takamimusubi]]
* [[Kamimusubi]]
* [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]]

{{Expand language|langcode=ja|otherarticle=東京大神宮}}{{Infobox religious building | name = Tokyo Daijingu | religious_affiliation = Shinto | image = File:Shrine, Tokyo Daijingu - Chiyoda, Tokyo, Japan - DSC04748.jpg | location = Address : 2-4-1, Fujimi, Chiyoda-ku, Tokyo, 102-0071 Japan | website = http://www.tokyodaijingu.or.jp/index.html }} '''Tokyo Daijingu''' is a shrine located in [[Tokyo]].<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|last=Bureau|first=Tokyo Convention & Visitors|title=Tokyo Daijingu Shrine|url=https://www.gotokyo.org/en/spot/42/index.html|access-date=2021-11-07|website=The Official Tokyo Travel Guide, GO TOKYO|language=en}}</ref> The shrine is also called ''O-Ise-sama in Tokyo'' because of the deities enshrined there.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=English - Tokyo Daijingu|url=http://www.tokyodaijingu.or.jp/english/|access-date=2021-11-07|website=www.tokyodaijingu.or.jp|language=ja}}</ref>
It is one of the top five shrines in Tokyo.<ref name=":1" />
== History == The shrine was built in the early [[Meiji (era)|Meiji period]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Hardacre|first=Helen|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=nP7vr5yaaZIC&newbks=0&printsec=frontcover&pg=PA89&dq=Tokyo+Daijingu&hl=en|title=Shinto and the State, 1868-1988|date=1989|publisher=Princeton University Press|isbn=978-0-691-02052-5|language=en}}</ref>{{Rp|89}} so people in Tokyo could worship the deities enshrined at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Grand Shrine of Ise]] from afar. Back then it was originally called ''Hibiya Daijingu''.<ref name=":0" />
In 1901, a webbing too place at the shrine being this was the first [[Shinto wedding]] for people in urban areas.<ref>{{Cite book|last=de-Gaia|first=Susan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=jt91DwAAQBAJ&pg=RA1-PA286&dq=Tokyo+Daijingu+history&hl=en&newbks=1&newbks_redir=0&source=gb_mobile_search&sa=X&ved=2ahUKEwjk05uUt4b0AhXkgnIEHdlhBAQQ6AF6BAgHEAM|title=Encyclopedia of Women in World Religions: Faith and Culture across History [2 volumes]|date=2018-11-16|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-4408-4850-6|language=en}}</ref>{{Rp|286}}
After the [[1923 Great Kantō earthquake|Kanto Earthquake]], the shrine was was moved to [[Iidabashi]] in 1928 and renamed to ''Iidabashi Daijingu''. Then after [[World War II|World War 2]] the place changed its name to Tokyo Daijingu.<ref name=":0" />
== Enshrined kami == Deities enshrined here include.<ref name=":0" />
* [[Amaterasu]]
* [[Toyoukebime|Toyouke-no-Ohkami]]
* [[Ame-no-Minakanushi|Ameno-Minakanushi]]
* [[Takamimusubi]]
* [[Kamimusubi]]
* [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]]

{{Expand language|langcode=ja|otherarticle=東京大神宮|date=November 2021}}{{Infobox religious building | name = Tokyo Daijingu | religious_affiliation = Shinto | image = File:Shrine, Tokyo Daijingu - Chiyoda, Tokyo, Japan - DSC04748.jpg | location = Address : 2-4-1, Fujimi, Chiyoda-ku, Tokyo, 102-0071 Japan | website = http://www.tokyodaijingu.or.jp/index.html }} '''Tokyo Daijingu''' is a shrine located in [[Tokyo]].<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|last=Bureau|first=Tokyo Convention & Visitors|title=Tokyo Daijingu Shrine|url=https://www.gotokyo.org/en/spot/42/index.html|access-date=2021-11-07|website=The Official Tokyo Travel Guide, GO TOKYO|language=en}}</ref> The shrine is also called ''O-Ise-sama in Tokyo'' because of the deities enshrined there.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=English - Tokyo Daijingu|url=http://www.tokyodaijingu.or.jp/english/|access-date=2021-11-07|website=www.tokyodaijingu.or.jp|language=ja}}</ref>
It is one of the top five shrines in Tokyo.<ref name=":1" />
== History == The shrine was built in the early [[Meiji (era)|Meiji period]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Hardacre|first=Helen|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=nP7vr5yaaZIC&dq=Tokyo+Daijingu&pg=PA89|title=Shinto and the State, 1868-1988|date=1989|publisher=Princeton University Press|isbn=978-0-691-02052-5|language=en}}</ref>{{Rp|89}} so people in Tokyo could worship the deities enshrined at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Grand Shrine of Ise]] from afar. Back then it was originally called ''Hibiya Daijingu''.<ref name=":0" />
In 1901, a webbing too place at the shrine being the first [[Shinto wedding]] for people in urban areas.<ref>{{Cite book|last=de-Gaia|first=Susan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=jt91DwAAQBAJ&dq=Tokyo+Daijingu+history&pg=RA1-PA286|title=Encyclopedia of Women in World Religions: Faith and Culture across History [2 volumes]|date=2018-11-16|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-4408-4850-6|language=en}}</ref>{{Rp|286}}
After the [[1923 Great Kantō earthquake|Kanto Earthquake]], the shrine was moved to [[Iidabashi]] in 1928 and renamed to ''Iidabashi Daijingu''. Then after [[World War II|World War 2]] the place changed its name to Tokyo Daijingu.<ref name=":0" /> [[Category:Beppo Shrines]]
== Enshrined kami == Deities enshrined here include.<ref name=":0" />
* [[Amaterasu]]
* [[Toyoukebime|Toyouke-no-Ohkami]]
* [[Ame-no-Minakanushi|Ameno-Minakanushi]]
* [[Takamimusubi]]
* [[Kamimusubi]]
* [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]]
== References == {{reflist}}
{{coord|35.7000|139.7469|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title}}
[[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]]
{{Japan-religious-struct-stub}} {{Shinto-stub}}

[[Ame-no-Minakanushi]]
[[Takamimusubi]]
[[Kamimusubi]] | website = http://www.tokyodaijingu.or.jp/index.html }} '''Tokyo Daijingu''' is a shrine located in [[Tokyo]].{{Cite web|last=Bureau|first=Tokyo Convention & Visitors|title=Tokyo Daijingu Shrine|url=https://www.gotokyo.org/en/spot/42/index.html|access-date=2021-11-07|website=The Official Tokyo Travel Guide, GO TOKYO|language=en}} The shrine is also called ''O-Ise-sama in Tokyo'' because of the deities enshrined there.{{Cite web|title=English - Tokyo Daijingu|url=http://www.tokyodaijingu.or.jp/english/|access-date=2021-11-07|website=www.tokyodaijingu.or.jp|language=ja}} It is one of the top five shrines in Tokyo. == History == The shrine was built in the early [[Meiji (era)|Meiji period]]{{Cite book|last=Hardacre|first=Helen|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=nP7vr5yaaZIC&dq=Tokyo+Daijingu&pg=PA89|title=Shinto and the State, 1868-1988|date=1989|publisher=Princeton University Press|isbn=978-0-691-02052-5|language=en}}{{Rp|89}} by [[Jingu-kyo]]{{Cite book |last=村上 |first=重良 |title= |date=August 2007 |publisher=講談社 |isbn=978-4061598324 |series=講談社学術文庫 |pages=117-118 |script-title=ja:天皇制国家と宗教 |author-link=村上重良}} so people in Tokyo could worship the deities enshrined at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Grand Shrine of Ise]] from afar. Back then it was originally called ''Hibiya Daijingu''. In 1901, a wedding took place at the shrine, being the first [[Shinto wedding]] held in an urban area.{{Cite book|last=de-Gaia|first=Susan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=jt91DwAAQBAJ&dq=Tokyo+Daijingu+history&pg=RA1-PA286|title=Encyclopedia of Women in World Religions: Faith and Culture across History [2 volumes]|date=2018-11-16|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-4408-4850-6|language=en}}{{Rp|286}} After the [[1923 Great Kantō earthquake|Kanto Earthquake]], the shrine was moved to [[Iidabashi]] in 1928 and renamed to ''Iidabashi Daijingu''. Then after [[World War II|World War 2]], the place changed its name to Tokyo Daijingu. [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] == Enshrined kami == Deities enshrined here include. * [[Amaterasu]] * [[Toyoukebime|Toyouke-no-Ohkami]] * [[Ame-no-Minakanushi|Ameno-Minakanushi]] * [[Takamimusubi]] * [[Kamimusubi]] * [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] == References == {{reflist}} {{coord|35.7000|139.7469|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Shinmei Shrines]] {{Japan-religious-struct-stub}} {{Shinto-stub}}

Back then it was originally called ''Hibiya Daijingu''. In 1901, a wedding took place at the shrine, being the first [[Shinto wedding]] held in an urban area.{{Cite book|last=de-Gaia|first=Susan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=jt91DwAAQBAJ&dq=Tokyo+Daijingu+history&pg=RA1-PA286|title=Encyclopedia of Women in World Religions: Faith and Culture across History [2 volumes]|date=2018-11-16|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-4408-4850-6|language=en}}{{Rp|286}} After the [[1923 Great Kantō earthquake|Kanto Earthquake]], the shrine was moved to [[Iidabashi]] in 1928 and renamed to ''Iidabashi Daijingu''. Then after [[World War II|World War 2]], the place changed its name to Tokyo Daijingu. [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] == Enshrined kami == Deities enshrined here include. * [[Amaterasu]] * [[Toyoukebime|Toyouke-no-Ohkami]] * [[Ame-no-Minakanushi|Ameno-Minakanushi]] * [[Takamimusubi]] * [[Kamimusubi]] * [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] == References == {{reflist}} {{coord|35.7000|139.7469|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Shinmei Shrines]] {{Japan-religious-struct-stub}} {{Shinto-stub}}

Then after [[World War II|World War 2]], the place changed its name to Tokyo Daijingu. == Enshrined kami == Deities enshrined here include. * [[Amaterasu]] * [[Toyoukebime|Toyouke-no-Ohkami]] * [[Ame-no-Minakanushi|Ameno-Minakanushi]] * [[Takamimusubi]] * [[Kamimusubi]] * [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] == References == {{Reflist}} {{Shinmei shrines}} {{Authority control}} {{coord|35.7000|139.7469|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title}} [[category:kk]][[category:Beppyo shrines]] [[category:kk]][[category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[category:kk]][[category:Shinmei shrines]] [[category:kk]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[category:mm]] [[category:kk]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[category:mm]] [[category:kk]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[category:mm]] [[en:Tokyo Daijingu]][[category:kk]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[category:mm]] [[category:kk]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[category:mm]] [[Category:ファイル]] [[en:Tokyo Daijingu]] [[en:Tokyo Daijingu]] {{Japan-religious-struct-stub}} {{Shinto-stub}}

[[Ukemochi]]
[[Ame-no-Minakanushi]]
[[Takamimusubi]]
[[Kamimusubi]] | website = http://www.tokyodaijingu.or.jp/index.html }} '''Tokyo Daijingu''' is a shrine located in [[Tokyo]].{{Cite web|last=Bureau|first=Tokyo Convention & Visitors|title=Tokyo Daijingu Shrine|url=https://www.gotokyo.org/en/spot/42/index.html|access-date=2021-11-07|website=The Official Tokyo Travel Guide, GO TOKYO|language=en}} The shrine is also called ''O-Ise-sama in Tokyo'' because of the deities enshrined there.{{Cite web|title=English - Tokyo Daijingu|url=http://www.tokyodaijingu.or.jp/english/|access-date=2021-11-07|website=www.tokyodaijingu.or.jp|language=ja}} It is one of the top five shrines in Tokyo. == History == The shrine was built in the early [[Meiji (era)|Meiji period]]{{Cite book|last=Hardacre|first=Helen|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=nP7vr5yaaZIC&dq=Tokyo+Daijingu&pg=PA89|title=Shinto and the State, 1868-1988|date=1989|publisher=Princeton University Press|isbn=978-0-691-02052-5|language=en}}{{Rp|89}} by [[Jingu-kyo]]{{Cite book |last=村上 |first=重良 |title= |date=August 2007 |publisher=講談社 |isbn=978-4061598324 |series=講談社学術文庫 |pages=117–118 |script-title=ja:天皇制国家と宗教 |author-link=村上重良}} so people in Tokyo could worship the deities enshrined at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Grand Shrine of Ise]] from afar. Back then it was originally called ''Hibiya Daijingu''. In 1901, a wedding took place at the shrine, being the first [[Shinto wedding]] held in an urban area.{{Cite book|last=de-Gaia|first=Susan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=jt91DwAAQBAJ&dq=Tokyo+Daijingu+history&pg=RA1-PA286|title=Encyclopedia of Women in World Religions: Faith and Culture across History [2 volumes]|date=2018-11-16|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-4408-4850-6|language=en}}{{Rp|286}} After the [[1923 Great Kantō earthquake|Kanto Earthquake]], the shrine was moved to [[Iidabashi]] in 1928 and renamed to ''Iidabashi Daijingu''. Then after [[World War II|World War 2]], the place changed its name to Tokyo Daijingu. == Enshrined kami == Deities enshrined here include. * [[Amaterasu]] * [[Toyoukebime|Toyouke-no-Ohkami]] * [[Ame-no-Minakanushi|Ameno-Minakanushi]] * [[Takamimusubi]] * [[Kamimusubi]] * [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] == References == {{Reflist}} {{Shinmei shrines}} {{Authority control}} {{coord|35.7000|139.7469|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title}} [[category:kk]][[category:Beppyo shrines]] [[category:kk]][[category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[category:kk]][[category:Shinmei shrines]] [[category:kk]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[category:mm]] [[category:kk]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[category:mm]] [[category:kk]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[category:mm]] [[en:Tokyo Daijingu]][[category:kk]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[category:mm]] [[category:kk]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[category:mm]] [[Category:ファイル]] [[Category:mass editing for the third subcategory]] [[en:Tokyo Daijingu]] [[en:Tokyo Daijingu]] {{Japan-religious-struct-stub}} {{Shinto-stub}}

Then after [[World War II|World War 2]], the place changed its name to Tokyo Daijingu. == Enshrined kami == Deities enshrined here include. * [[Amaterasu]] * [[Toyoukebime|Toyouke-no-Ohkami]] * [[Ame-no-Minakanushi|Ameno-Minakanushi]] * [[Takamimusubi]] * [[Kamimusubi]] * [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] == References == {{Reflist}} {{Shinmei shrines}} {{Authority control}} {{coord|35.7000|139.7469|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title}} [[category:kk]][[category:Beppyo shrines]] [[category:kk]][[category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[category:kk]][[category:Shinmei shrines]] [[category:kk]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]][[en:Tokyo Daijingu]]]] [[category:mm]] [[category:kk]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]][[en:Tokyo Daijingu]]]] [[category:mm]] [[category:kk]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]][[en:Tokyo Daijingu]]]] [[category:mm]] [[en:Tokyo Daijingu]][[category:kk]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]][[en:Tokyo Daijingu]]]] [[category:mm]] [[category:kk]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]][[en:Tokyo Daijingu]]]] [[category:mm]] [[category:mass editing for the third subcategory]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Tokyo Daijingu]][[Category:dd]] [[en:Tokyo Daijingu]] [[en:Tokyo Daijingu]] {{Japan-religious-struct-stub}} {{Shinto-stub}}

Then after [[World War II|World War 2]], the place changed its name to Tokyo Daijingu. == Enshrined kami == Deities enshrined here include. * [[Amaterasu]] * [[Toyoukebime|Toyouke-no-Ohkami]] * [[Ame-no-Minakanushi|Ameno-Minakanushi]] * [[Takamimusubi]] * [[Kamimusubi]] * [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] == References == {{Reflist}} {{Shinmei shrines}} {{Authority control}} {{coord|35.7000|139.7469|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title}} [[category:Beppyo shrines]] [[category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[category:Shinmei shrines]] [[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]][[en:Tokyo Daijingu]]]] [[category:mm]] [[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]][[en:Tokyo Daijingu]]]] [[category:mm]] [[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]][[en:Tokyo Daijingu]]]] [[category:mm]] [[en:Tokyo Daijingu]][[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]][[en:Tokyo Daijingu]]]] [[category:mm]] [[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]][[en:Tokyo Daijingu]]]] [[category:mm]] [[category:mass editing for the third subcategory]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Tokyo Daijingu]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:making categorization show up on newly imported categories and templates]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[en:Tokyo Daijingu]] [[en:Tokyo Daijingu]] {{Japan-religious-struct-stub}} {{Shinto-stub}}

[[Ame-no-Minakanushi]]
[[Takamimusubi]]
[[Kamimusubi]] | website = http://www.tokyodaijingu.or.jp/index.html }} '''Tokyo Daijingu''' is a shrine located in [[Tokyo]].{{Cite web|last=Bureau|first=Tokyo Convention & Visitors|title=Tokyo Daijingu Shrine|url=https://www.gotokyo.org/en/spot/42/index.html|access-date=2021-11-07|website=The Official Tokyo Travel Guide, GO TOKYO|language=en}} The shrine is also called ''O-Ise-sama in Tokyo'' because of the deities enshrined there.{{Cite web|title=English - Tokyo Daijingu|url=http://www.tokyodaijingu.or.jp/english/|access-date=2021-11-07|website=www.tokyodaijingu.or.jp|language=ja}} It is one of the top five shrines in Tokyo. == History == The shrine was built in the early [[Meiji (era)|Meiji period]]{{Cite book|last=Hardacre|first=Helen|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=nP7vr5yaaZIC&dq=Tokyo+Daijingu&pg=PA89|title=Shinto and the State, 1868-1988|date=1989|publisher=Princeton University Press|isbn=978-0-691-02052-5|language=en}}{{Rp|89}} by [[Jingu-kyo]]{{Cite book |last=村上 |first=重良 |title= |date=August 2007 |publisher=講談社 |isbn=978-4061598324 |series=講談社学術文庫 |pages=117–118 |script-title=ja:天皇制国家と宗教 |author-link=村上重良}} so people in Tokyo could worship the deities enshrined at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Grand Shrine of Ise]] from afar. Back then it was originally called ''Hibiya Daijingu''. In 1901, a wedding took place at the shrine, being the first [[Shinto wedding]] held in an urban area.{{Cite book|last=de-Gaia|first=Susan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=jt91DwAAQBAJ&dq=Tokyo+Daijingu+history&pg=RA1-PA286|title=Encyclopedia of Women in World Religions: Faith and Culture across History [2 volumes]|date=2018-11-16|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-4408-4850-6|language=en}}{{Rp|286}} After the [[1923 Great Kantō earthquake|Kanto Earthquake]], the shrine was moved to [[Iidabashi]] in 1928 and renamed to ''Iidabashi Daijingu''. Then after [[World War II|World War 2]], the place changed its name to Tokyo Daijingu. == Enshrined kami == Deities enshrined here include. * [[Amaterasu]] * [[Toyoukebime|Toyouke-no-Ohkami]] * [[Ame-no-Minakanushi|Ameno-Minakanushi]] * [[Takamimusubi]] * [[Kamimusubi]] * [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] == References == {{Reflist}} {{Shinmei shrines}} [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]][[en:Tokyo Daijingu]] {{coord|35.7000|139.7469|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title}} [[category:Beppyo shrines]] [[category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[category:Shinmei shrines]] [[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]][[en:Tokyo Daijingu]]]] [[category:mm]] [[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]][[en:Tokyo Daijingu]]]] [[category:mm]] [[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]][[en:Tokyo Daijingu]]]] [[category:mm]] [[en:Tokyo Daijingu]][[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]][[en:Tokyo Daijingu]]]] [[category:mm]] [[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]][[en:Tokyo Daijingu]]]] [[category:mm]] [[category:mass editing for the third subcategory]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Tokyo Daijingu]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:making categorization show up on newly imported categories and templates]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[en:Tokyo Daijingu]] [[en:Tokyo Daijingu]] {{Japan-religious-struct-stub}} {{Shinto-stub}}

Then after [[World War II|World War 2]], the place changed its name to Tokyo Daijingu. == Enshrined kami == Deities enshrined here include. * [[Amaterasu]] * [[Toyoukebime|Toyouke-no-Ohkami]] * [[Ame-no-Minakanushi|Ameno-Minakanushi]] * [[Takamimusubi]] * [[Kamimusubi]] * [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] == References == {{Reflist}} {{Shinmei shrines}} [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]][[en:Tokyo Daijingu]] {{coord|35.7000|139.7469|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Shinmei shrines]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]][[en:Tokyo Daijingu]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]][[en:Tokyo Daijingu]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]][[en:Tokyo Daijingu]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[en:Tokyo Daijingu]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]][[en:Tokyo Daijingu]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]][[en:Tokyo Daijingu]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:mass editing for the third subCategory]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Tokyo Daijingu]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:making categorization show up on newly imported categories and templates]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[en:Tokyo Daijingu]] [[en:Tokyo Daijingu]] {{Japan-religious-struct-stub}} {{Shinto-stub}}

In 1901, a wedding took place at the shrine, being the first [[Shinto wedding]] held in an urban area.{{Cite book|last=de-Gaia|first=Susan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=jt91DwAAQBAJ&dq=Tokyo+Daijingu+history&pg=RA1-PA286|title=Encyclopedia of Women in World Religions: Faith and Culture across History [2 volumes]|date=2018-11-16|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-4408-4850-6|language=en}}{{Rp|286}} After the [[1923 Great Kantō earthquake|Kanto Earthquake]], the shrine was moved to [[Iidabashi]] in 1928 and renamed to ''Iidabashi Daijingu''. Then after [[World War II|World War 2]], the place changed its name to Tokyo Daijingu. == Enshrined kami == Deities enshrined here include. * [[Amaterasu]] * [[Toyoukebime|Toyouke-no-Ohkami]] * [[Ame-no-Minakanushi|Ameno-Minakanushi]] * [[Takamimusubi]] * [[Kamimusubi]] * [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] == References == {{Reflist}} {{Shinmei shrines}} [[category:Pages that used to use Authority control]][[en:Tokyo Daijingu]] {{coord|35.7000|139.7469|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[category:Shinmei shrines]] [[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]][[en:Tokyo Daijingu]]]] [[category:mm]] [[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]][[en:Tokyo Daijingu]]]] [[category:mm]] [[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]][[en:Tokyo Daijingu]]]] [[category:mm]] [[en:Tokyo Daijingu]][[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]][[en:Tokyo Daijingu]]]] [[category:mm]] [[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]][[en:Tokyo Daijingu]]]] [[category:mm]] [[category:mass editing for the third subcategory]] [[category:dd]][[category:Tokyo Daijingu]][[category:dd]] [[category:making categorization show up on newly imported categories and templates]] [[category:1 revision imported]] [[category:Bump-01-21]] [[category:Bump-01-21]] [[category:bump 1-26]] [[category:kk]][[en:Tokyo Daijingu]][[category:kk]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokyo Daijingu]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Tokyo Daijingu]][[Category:qq]] [[en:Tokyo Daijingu]] [[en:Tokyo Daijingu]] {{Japan-religious-struct-stub}} {{Shinto-stub}}

Then after [[World War II|World War 2]], the place changed its name to Tokyo Daijingu. == Enshrined kami == Deities enshrined here include. * [[Amaterasu]] * [[Toyoukebime|Toyouke-no-Ohkami]] * [[Ame-no-Minakanushi|Ameno-Minakanushi]] * [[Takamimusubi]] * [[Kamimusubi]] * [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] == References == {{Reflist}} {{Shinmei shrines}} [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]][[en:Tokyo Daijingu]] {{coord|35.7000|139.7469|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shinmei shrines needed to be moved in template]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Shinmei shrines]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]][[Category:qq 6]] [[Category:qq--qq]] [[en:Tokyo Daijingu]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]][[Category:qq 6]] [[Category:qq--qq]] [[en:Tokyo Daijingu]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]][[Category:qq 6]] [[Category:qq--qq]] [[en:Tokyo Daijingu]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[en:Tokyo Daijingu]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]][[Category:qq 6]] [[Category:qq--qq]] [[en:Tokyo Daijingu]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]][[Category:qq 6]] [[Category:qq--qq]] [[en:Tokyo Daijingu]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:mass editing for the third subCategory]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Tokyo Daijingu]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:making categorization show up on newly imported categories and templates]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Tokyo Daijingu]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokyo Daijingu]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Tokyo Daijingu]][[Category:qq]] [[en:Tokyo Daijingu]] [[en:Tokyo Daijingu]] {{Japan-religious-struct-stub}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]]

{{Short description|Shrine in Tokyo, Japan}} {{Multiple issues| {{Expand Japanese|topic=struct|東京大神宮|date=November 2021}} {{Expand Polish|topic=struct| | date = 5 2023 | fa = yes }} }} {{Infobox religious building | name = Tokyo Daijingu | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = File:Shrine, Tokyo Daijingu - Chiyoda, Tokyo, Japan - DSC04748.jpg | location = Address : 2-4-1, Fujimi, Chiyoda-ku, Tokyo, 102-0071 Japan | deity = [[Amaterasu]]
[[Ukemochi]]
[[Ame-no-Minakanushi]]
[[Takamimusubi]]
[[Kamimusubi]] | website = http://www.tokyodaijingu.or.jp/index.html }} '''Tokyo Daijingu''' is a shrine located in [[Tokyo]].{{Cite web|last=Bureau|first=Tokyo Convention & Visitors|title=Tokyo Daijingu Shrine|url=https://www.gotokyo.org/en/spot/42/index.html|access-date=2021-11-07|website=The Official Tokyo Travel Guide, GO TOKYO|language=en}} The shrine is also called ''O-Ise-sama in Tokyo'' because of the deities enshrined there.{{Cite web|title=English - Tokyo Daijingu|url=http://www.tokyodaijingu.or.jp/english/|access-date=2021-11-07|website=www.tokyodaijingu.or.jp|language=ja}} It is one of the top five shrines in Tokyo. == History == The shrine was built in the early [[Meiji (era)|Meiji period]]{{Cite book|last=Hardacre|first=Helen|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=nP7vr5yaaZIC&dq=Tokyo+Daijingu&pg=PA89|title=Shinto and the State, 1868-1988|date=1989|publisher=Princeton University Press|isbn=978-0-691-02052-5|language=en}}{{Rp|89}} by [[Jingu-kyo]]{{Cite book |last=村上 |first=重良 |title= |date=August 2007 |publisher=講談社 |isbn=978-4061598324 |series=講談社学術文庫 |pages=117–118 |script-title=ja:天皇制国家と宗教 |author-link=村上重良}} so people in Tokyo could worship the deities enshrined at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Grand Shrine of Ise]] from afar. Back then it was originally called ''Hibiya Daijingu''. In 1901, a wedding took place at the shrine, being the first [[Shinto wedding]] held in an urban area.{{Cite book|last=de-Gaia|first=Susan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=jt91DwAAQBAJ&dq=Tokyo+Daijingu+history&pg=RA1-PA286|title=Encyclopedia of Women in World Religions: Faith and Culture across History [2 volumes]|date=2018-11-16|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-4408-4850-6|language=en}}{{Rp|286}} After the [[1923 Great Kantō earthquake|Kanto Earthquake]], the shrine was moved to [[Iidabashi]] in 1928 and renamed to ''Iidabashi Daijingu''. Then after [[World War II|World War 2]], the place changed its name to Tokyo Daijingu. == Enshrined kami == Deities enshrined here include. * [[Amaterasu]] * [[Toyoukebime|Toyouke-no-Ohkami]] * [[Ame-no-Minakanushi|Ameno-Minakanushi]] * [[Takamimusubi]] * [[Kamimusubi]] * [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] == References == {{Reflist}} {{Shinmei shrines}} [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority [[Kamimusubi]] | website = http://www.tokyodaijingu.or.jp/index.html }} '''Tokyo Daijingu''' is a shrine located in [[Tokyo]].{{Cite web|last=Bureau|first=Tokyo Convention & Visitors|title=Tokyo Daijingu Shrine|url=https://www.gotokyo.org/en/spot/42/index.html|access-date=2021-11-07|website=The Official Tokyo Travel Guide, GO TOKYO|language=en}} The shrine is also called ''O-Ise-sama in Tokyo'' because of the deities enshrined there.{{Cite web|title=English - Tokyo Daijingu|url=http://www.tokyodaijingu.or.jp/english/|access-date=2021-11-07|website=www.tokyodaijingu.or.jp|language=ja}} It is one of the top five shrines in Tokyo. == History == The shrine was built in the early [[Meiji (era)|Meiji period]]{{Cite book|last=Hardacre|first=Helen|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=nP7vr5yaaZIC&dq=Tokyo+Daijingu&pg=PA89|title=Shinto and the State, 1868-1988|date=1989|publisher=Princeton University Press|isbn=978-0-691-02052-5|language=en}}{{Rp|89}} by [[Jingu-kyo]]{{Cite book |last=村上 |first=重良 |title= |date=August 2007 |publisher=講談社 |isbn=978-4061598324 |series=講談社学術文庫 |pages=117–118 |script-title=ja:天皇制国家と宗教 |author-link=村上重良}} so people in Tokyo could worship the deities enshrined at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Grand Shrine of Ise]] from afar. Back then it was originally called ''Hibiya Daijingu''. In 1901, a wedding took place at the shrine, being the first [[Shinto wedding]] held in an urban area.{{Cite book|last=de-Gaia|first=Susan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=jt91DwAAQBAJ&dq=Tokyo+Daijingu+history&pg=RA1-PA286|title=Encyclopedia of Women in World Religions: Faith and Culture across History [2 volumes]|date=2018-11-16|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-4408-4850-6|language=en}}{{Rp|286}} After the [[1923 Great Kantō earthquake|Kanto Earthquake]], the shrine was moved to [[Iidabashi]] in 1928 and renamed to ''Iidabashi Daijingu''. Then after [[World War II|World War 2]], the place changed its name to Tokyo Daijingu. == Enshrined kami == Deities enshrined here include. * [[Amaterasu]] * [[Toyoukebime|Toyouke-no-Ohkami]] * [[Ame-no-Minakanushi|Ameno-Minakanushi]] * [[Takamimusubi]] * [[Kamimusubi]] * [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] == References == {{Reflist}} {{Shinmei shrines}} [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority [[Ukemochi]]
[[Ame-no-Minakanushi]]
[[Takamimusubi]]
[[Kamimusubi]] | website = http://www.tokyodaijingu.or.jp/index.html }} '''Tokyo Daijingu''' is a shrine located in [[Tokyo]].{{Cite web|last=Bureau|first=Tokyo Convention & Visitors|title=Tokyo Daijingu Shrine|url=https://www.gotokyo.org/en/spot/42/index.html|access-date=2021-11-07|website=The Official Tokyo Travel Guide, GO TOKYO|language=en}} The shrine is also called ''O-Ise-sama in Tokyo'' because of the deities enshrined there.{{Cite web|title=English - Tokyo Daijingu|url=http://www.tokyodaijingu.or.jp/english/|access-date=2021-11-07|website=www.tokyodaijingu.or.jp|language=ja}} It is one of the top five shrines in Tokyo. == History == The shrine was built in the early [[Meiji (era)|Meiji period]]{{Cite book|last=Hardacre|first=Helen|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=nP7vr5yaaZIC&dq=Tokyo+Daijingu&pg=PA89|title=Shinto and the State, 1868-1988|date=1989|publisher=Princeton University Press|isbn=978-0-691-02052-5|language=en}}{{Rp|89}} by [[Jingu-kyo]]{{Cite book |last=村上 |first=重良 |title= |date=August 2007 |publisher=講談社 |isbn=978-4061598324 |series=講談社学術文庫 |pages=117–118 |script-title=ja:天皇制国家と宗教 |author-link=村上重良}} so people in Tokyo could worship the deities enshrined at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Grand Shrine of Ise]] from afar. Back then it was originally called ''Hibiya Daijingu''. In 1901, a wedding took place at the shrine, being the first [[Shinto wedding]] held in an urban area.{{Cite book|last=de-Gaia|first=Susan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=jt91DwAAQBAJ&dq=Tokyo+Daijingu+history&pg=RA1-PA286|title=Encyclopedia of Women in World Religions: Faith and Culture across History [2 volumes]|date=2018-11-16|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-4408-4850-6|language=en}}{{Rp|286}} After the [[1923 Great Kantō earthquake|Kanto Earthquake]], the shrine was moved to [[Iidabashi]] in 1928 and renamed to ''Iidabashi Daijingu''. Then after [[World War II|World War 2]], the place changed its name to Tokyo Daijingu. == Enshrined kami == Deities enshrined here include. * [[Amaterasu]] * [[Toyoukebime|Toyouke-no-Ohkami]] * [[Ame-no-Minakanushi|Ameno-Minakanushi]] * [[Takamimusubi]] * [[Kamimusubi]] * [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] == References == {{Reflist}} {{Shinmei shrines}} [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority {{Short description|Shrine in Tokyo, Japan}} {{Multiple issues| {{Expand Japanese|topic=struct|東京大神宮|date=November 2021}} {{Expand Polish|topic=struct| | date = 5 2023 | fa = yes }} }} {{Infobox religious building | name = Tokyo Daijingu | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = File:Shrine, Tokyo Daijingu - Chiyoda, Tokyo, Japan - DSC04748.jpg | location = Address : 2-4-1, Fujimi, Chiyoda-ku, Tokyo, 102-0071 Japan | deity = [[Amaterasu]]
[[Ukemochi]]
[[Ame-no-Minakanushi]]
[[Takamimusubi]]
[[Kamimusubi]] | website = http://www.tokyodaijingu.or.jp/index.html }} '''Tokyo Daijingu''' is a shrine located in [[Tokyo]].{{Cite web|last=Bureau|first=Tokyo Convention & Visitors|title=Tokyo Daijingu Shrine|url=https://www.gotokyo.org/en/spot/42/index.html|access-date=2021-11-07|website=The Official Tokyo Travel Guide, GO TOKYO|language=en}} The shrine is also called ''O-Ise-sama in Tokyo'' because of the deities enshrined there.{{Cite web|title=English - Tokyo Daijingu|url=http://www.tokyodaijingu.or.jp/english/|access-date=2021-11-07|website=www.tokyodaijingu.or.jp|language=ja}} It is one of the top five shrines in Tokyo. == History == The shrine was built in the early [[Meiji (era)|Meiji period]]{{Cite book|last=Hardacre|first=Helen|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=nP7vr5yaaZIC&dq=Tokyo+Daijingu&pg=PA89|title=Shinto and the State, 1868-1988|date=1989|publisher=Princeton University Press|isbn=978-0-691-02052-5|language=en}}{{Rp|89}} by [[Jingu-kyo]]{{Cite book |last=村上 |first=重良 |title= |date=August 2007 |publisher=講談社 |isbn=978-4061598324 |series=講談社学術文庫 |pages=117–118 |script-title=ja:天皇制国家と宗教 |author-link=村上重良}} so people in Tokyo could worship the deities enshrined at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Grand Shrine of Ise]] from afar. Back then it was originally called ''Hibiya Daijingu''. In 1901, a wedding took place at the shrine, being the first [[Shinto wedding]] held in an urban area.{{Cite book|last=de-Gaia|first=Susan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=jt91DwAAQBAJ&dq=Tokyo+Daijingu+history&pg=RA1-PA286|title=Encyclopedia of Women in World Religions: Faith and Culture across History [2 volumes]|date=2018-11-16|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-4408-4850-6|language=en}}{{Rp|286}} After the [[1923 Great Kantō earthquake|Kanto Earthquake]], the shrine was moved to [[Iidabashi]] in 1928 and renamed to ''Iidabashi Daijingu''. Then after [[World War II|World War 2]], the place changed its name to Tokyo Daijingu. == Enshrined kami == Deities enshrined here include. * [[Amaterasu]] * [[Toyoukebime|Toyouke-no-Ohkami]] * [[Ame-no-Minakanushi|Ameno-Minakanushi]] * [[Takamimusubi]] * [[Kamimusubi]] * [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] == References == {{Reflist}} {{Shinmei shrines}} [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]][[en:Tokyo Daijingu]] {{coord|35.7000|139.7469|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shinmei shrines needed to be moved in template]] [[Kamimusubi]] | website = http://www.tokyodaijingu.or.jp/index.html }} '''Tokyo Daijingu''' is a shrine located in [[Tokyo]].{{Cite web|last=Bureau|first=Tokyo Convention & Visitors|title=Tokyo Daijingu Shrine|url=https://www.gotokyo.org/en/spot/42/index.html|access-date=2021-11-07|website=The Official Tokyo Travel Guide, GO TOKYO|language=en}} The shrine is also called ''O-Ise-sama in Tokyo'' because of the deities enshrined there.{{Cite web|title=English - Tokyo Daijingu|url=http://www.tokyodaijingu.or.jp/english/|access-date=2021-11-07|website=www.tokyodaijingu.or.jp|language=ja}} It is one of the top five shrines in Tokyo. == History == The shrine was built in the early [[Meiji (era)|Meiji period]]{{Cite book|last=Hardacre|first=Helen|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=nP7vr5yaaZIC&dq=Tokyo+Daijingu&pg=PA89|title=Shinto and the State, 1868-1988|date=1989|publisher=Princeton University Press|isbn=978-0-691-02052-5|language=en}}{{Rp|89}} by [[Jingu-kyo]]{{Cite book |last=村上 |first=重良 |title= |date=August 2007 |publisher=講談社 |isbn=978-4061598324 |series=講談社学術文庫 |pages=117–118 |script-title=ja:天皇制国家と宗教 |author-link=村上重良}} so people in Tokyo could worship the deities enshrined at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Grand Shrine of Ise]] from afar. Back then it was originally called ''Hibiya Daijingu''. In 1901, a wedding took place at the shrine, being the first [[Shinto wedding]] held in an urban area.{{Cite book|last=de-Gaia|first=Susan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=jt91DwAAQBAJ&dq=Tokyo+Daijingu+history&pg=RA1-PA286|title=Encyclopedia of Women in World Religions: Faith and Culture across History [2 volumes]|date=2018-11-16|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-4408-4850-6|language=en}}{{Rp|286}} After the [[1923 Great Kantō earthquake|Kanto Earthquake]], the shrine was moved to [[Iidabashi]] in 1928 and renamed to ''Iidabashi Daijingu''. Then after [[World War II|World War 2]], the place changed its name to Tokyo Daijingu. == Enshrined kami == Deities enshrined here include. * [[Amaterasu]] * [[Toyoukebime|Toyouke-no-Ohkami]] * | website               = http://www.tokyodaijingu.or.jp/index.html
| prefecture= {{ill|Fujimi|qid=Q3347253}}
| coordinates= {{Coord|35.69998333|N|139.74686944|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}
}}

'''Tokyo Daijingu''' is a shrine located in [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]].<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|last=Bureau|first=Tokyo Convention & Visitors|title=Tokyo Daijingu Shrine|url=https://www.gotokyo.org/en/spot/42/index.html|access-date=2021-11-07|website=The Official Tokyo Travel Guide, GO TOKYO|language=en}}</ref>  The shrine is also called ''O-Ise-sama in Tokyo'' because of the deities enshrined there.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=English - Tokyo Daijingu|url=http://www.tokyodaijingu.or.jp/english/|access-date=2021-11-07|website=www.tokyodaijingu.or.jp|language=ja}}</ref> It is one of the top five shrines in Tokyo.<ref name=":1" />

== History ==
The shrine was built in the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Hardacre|first=Helen|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=nP7vr5yaaZIC&dq=Tokyo+Daijingu&pg=PA89|title=Shinto and the State, 1868-1988|date=1989|publisher=Princeton University Press|isbn=978-0-691-02052-5|language=en}}</ref>{{Rp|89}} by [[Jingūkyō|Jingu-kyo]]<ref>{{Cite book |last=村上 |first=重良 |title= |date=August 2007 |publisher=講談社 |isbn=978-4061598324 |series=講談社学術文庫 |pages=117–118 |script-title=ja:天皇制国家と宗教 |author-link=村上重良}}</ref> so people in Tokyo could worship the deities enshrined at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Grand Shrine of Ise]] from afar. Back then it was originally called ''Hibiya Daijingu''.<ref name=":0" />

In 1901, a wedding took place at the shrine, being the first [[:en:Shinto wedding|Shinto wedding]] held in an urban area.<ref>{{Cite book|last=de-Gaia|first=Susan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=jt91DwAAQBAJ&dq=Tokyo+Daijingu+history&pg=RA1-PA286|title=Encyclopedia of Women in World Religions: Faith and Culture across History [2 volumes]|date=2018-11-16|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-4408-4850-6|language=en}}</ref>{{Rp|286}}

After the [[:en:1923 Great Kantō earthquake|Kanto Earthquake]], the shrine was moved to [[:en:Iidabashi|Iidabashi]] in 1928 and renamed to ''Iidabashi Daijingu''. Then after [[:en:Pacific War|World War 2]], the place changed its name to Tokyo Daijingu.<ref name=":0" />

== Enshrined kami ==
Deities enshrined here include.<ref name=":0" />

* [[Amaterasu]]
* [[:en:Toyouke-hime|Toyouke-no-Ohkami]]
* [[Ame-no-Minakanushi|Ameno-Minakanushi]]
* [[Takamimusubi]]
* [[Kamimusubi]]
* [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]]

Back then it was originally called ''Hibiya Daijingu''.<ref name=":0" />

In 1901, a wedding took place at the shrine, being the first [[:en:Shinto wedding|Shinto wedding]] held in an urban area.<ref>{{Cite book|last=de-Gaia|first=Susan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=jt91DwAAQBAJ&dq=Tokyo+Daijingu+history&pg=RA1-PA286|title=Encyclopedia of Women in World Religions: Faith and Culture across History [2 volumes]|date=2018-11-16|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-4408-4850-6|language=en}}</ref>{{Rp|286}}

After the [[:en:1923 Great Kantō earthquake|Kanto Earthquake]], the shrine was moved to [[:en:Iidabashi|Iidabashi]] in 1928 and renamed to ''Iidabashi Daijingu''. Then after [[:en:Pacific War|World War 2]], the place changed its name to Tokyo Daijingu.<ref name=":0" />

== Enshrined kami ==
Deities enshrined here include.<ref name=":0" />

* [[Amaterasu]]
* [[:en:Toyouke-hime|Toyouke-no-Ohkami]]

'''Tokyo Daijingu''' is a shrine located in [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]].<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|last=Bureau|first=Tokyo Convention & Visitors|title=Tokyo Daijingu Shrine|url=https://www.gotokyo.org/en/spot/42/index.html|access-date=2021-11-07|website=The Official Tokyo Travel Guide, GO TOKYO|language=en}}</ref>  The shrine is also called ''O-Ise-sama in Tokyo'' because of the deities enshrined there.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=English - Tokyo Daijingu|url=http://www.tokyodaijingu.or.jp/english/|access-date=2021-11-07|website=www.tokyodaijingu.or.jp|language=ja}}</ref> It is one of the top five shrines in Tokyo.<ref name=":1" />

== History ==
The shrine was built in the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Hardacre|first=Helen|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=nP7vr5yaaZIC&dq=Tokyo+Daijingu&pg=PA89|title=Shinto and the State, 1868-1988|date=1989|publisher=Princeton University Press|isbn=978-0-691-02052-5|language=en}}</ref>{{Rp|89}} by [[Jingūkyō|Jingu-kyo]]<ref>{{Cite book |last=村上 |first=重良 |title= |date=August 2007 |publisher=講談社 |isbn=978-4061598324 |series=講談社学術文庫 |pages=117–118 |script-title=ja:天皇制国家と宗教 |author-link=村上重良}}</ref> so people in Tokyo could worship the deities enshrined at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Grand Shrine of Ise]] from afar. Back then it was originally called ''Hibiya Daijingu''.<ref name=":0" />

In 1901, a wedding took place at the shrine, being the first [[:en:Shinto wedding|Shinto wedding]] held in an urban area.<ref>{{Cite book|last=de-Gaia|first=Susan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=jt91DwAAQBAJ&dq=Tokyo+Daijingu+history&pg=RA1-PA286|title=Encyclopedia of Women in World Religions: Faith and Culture across History [2 volumes]|date=2018-11-16|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-4408-4850-6|language=en}}</ref>{{Rp|286}}

After the [[:en:1923 Great Kantō earthquake|Kanto Earthquake]], the shrine was moved to [[:en:Iidabashi|Iidabashi]] in 1928 and renamed to ''Iidabashi Daijingu''. Then after [[:en:Pacific War|World War 2]], the place changed its name to Tokyo Daijingu.<ref name=":0" />

== Enshrined kami ==
Deities enshrined here include.<ref name=":0" />

* [[Amaterasu]]
* [[:en:Toyouke-hime|Toyouke-no-Ohkami]]
* [[Ame-no-Minakanushi|Ameno-Minakanushi]]
* [[Takamimusubi]]
* [[Kamimusubi]]
* [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]]

The shrine was built in the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Hardacre|first=Helen|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=nP7vr5yaaZIC&dq=Tokyo+Daijingu&pg=PA89|title=Shinto and the State, 1868-1988|date=1989|publisher=Princeton University Press|isbn=978-0-691-02052-5|language=en}}</ref>{{Rp|89}} by [[Jingūkyō|Jingu-kyo]]<ref>{{Cite book |last=村上 |first=重良 |title= |date=August 2007 |publisher=講談社 |isbn=978-4061598324 |series=講談社学術文庫 |pages=117–118 |script-title=ja:天皇制国家と宗教 |author-link=村上重良}}</ref> so people in Tokyo could worship the deities enshrined at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Grand Shrine of Ise]] from afar. After the [[:en:1923 Great Kantō earthquake|Kanto Earthquake]], the shrine was moved to [[:en:Iidabashi|Iidabashi]] in 1928 and renamed to ''Iidabashi Daijingu''. Then after [[:en:Pacific War|World War 2]], the place changed its name to Tokyo Daijingu.<ref name=":0" />

'''Tokyo Daijingu''' is a shrine located in [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]].<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|last=Bureau|first=Tokyo Convention & Visitors|title=Tokyo Daijingu Shrine|url=https://www.gotokyo.org/en/spot/42/index.html|access-date=2021-11-07|website=The Official Tokyo Travel Guide, GO TOKYO|language=en}}</ref>  The shrine is also called ''O-Ise-sama in Tokyo'' because of the deities enshrined there.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=English - Tokyo Daijingu|url=http://www.tokyodaijingu.or.jp/english/|access-date=2021-11-07|website=www.tokyodaijingu.or.jp|language=ja}}</ref> It is one of the top five shrines in Tokyo.<ref name=":1" />

== History ==
The shrine was built in the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Hardacre|first=Helen|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=nP7vr5yaaZIC&dq=Tokyo+Daijingu&pg=PA89|title=Shinto and the State, 1868-1988|date=1989|publisher=Princeton University Press|isbn=978-0-691-02052-5|language=en}}</ref>{{Rp|89}} by [[Jingūkyō|Jingu-kyo]]<ref>{{Cite book |last=村上 |first=重良 |title= |date=August 2007 |publisher=講談社 |isbn=978-4061598324 |series=講談社学術文庫 |pages=117–118 |script-title=ja:天皇制国家と宗教 |author-link=村上重良}}</ref> so people in Tokyo could worship the deities enshrined at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Grand Shrine of Ise]] from afar. Back then it was originally called ''Hibiya Daijingu''.<ref name=":0" />

In 1901, a wedding took place at the shrine, being the first [[:en:Shinto wedding|Shinto wedding]] held in an urban area.<ref>{{Cite book|last=de-Gaia|first=Susan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=jt91DwAAQBAJ&dq=Tokyo+Daijingu+history&pg=RA1-PA286|title=Encyclopedia of Women in World Religions: Faith and Culture across History [2 volumes]|date=2018-11-16|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-4408-4850-6|language=en}}</ref>{{Rp|286}}

After the [[:en:1923 Great Kantō earthquake|Kanto Earthquake]], the shrine was moved to [[:en:Iidabashi|Iidabashi]] in 1928 and renamed to ''Iidabashi Daijingu''. Then after [[:en:Pacific War|World War 2]], the place changed its name to Tokyo Daijingu.<ref name=":0" />

== Enshrined kami ==
Deities enshrined here include.<ref name=":0" />

* [[Amaterasu]]
* [[:en:Toyouke-hime|Toyouke-no-Ohkami]]
* [[Ame-no-Minakanushi|Ameno-Minakanushi]]
* [[Takamimusubi]]
* [[Kamimusubi]]
* [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]]

{{Infobox religious building
| name                  = Tokyo Daijingu
| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]
| image                 = File:Tokyo Daijingu 2019e.jpg
| location              = Address : 2-4-1, Fujimi, Chiyoda-ku, Tokyo, 102-0071 Japan
| deity                 = {{ill|Amaterasu|WD=Q455602}}; {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|WD=Q2983724}}
| website               = http://www.tokyodaijingu.or.jp/index.html
| prefecture= {{ill|Fujimi|WD=Q3347253}}
| coordinates= {{Coord|35.69998333|N|139.74686944|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}
}}

'''Tokyo Daijingu''' is a shrine located in [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]].<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|last=Bureau|first=Tokyo Convention & Visitors|title=Tokyo Daijingu Shrine|url=https://www.gotokyo.org/en/spot/42/index.html|access-date=2021-11-07|website=The Official Tokyo Travel Guide, GO TOKYO|language=en}}</ref>  The shrine is also called ''O-Ise-sama in Tokyo'' because of the deities enshrined there.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=English - Tokyo Daijingu|url=http://www.tokyodaijingu.or.jp/english/|access-date=2021-11-07|website=www.tokyodaijingu.or.jp|language=ja}}</ref> It is one of the top five shrines in Tokyo.<ref name=":1" />

== History ==
The shrine was built in the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Hardacre|first=Helen|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=nP7vr5yaaZIC&dq=Tokyo+Daijingu&pg=PA89|title=Shinto and the State, 1868-1988|date=1989|publisher=Princeton University Press|isbn=978-0-691-02052-5|language=en}}</ref>{{Rp|89}} by [[Jingūkyō|Jingu-kyo]]<ref>{{Cite book |last=村上 |first=重良 |title= |date=August 2007 |publisher=講談社 |isbn=978-4061598324 |series=講談社学術文庫 |pages=117–118 |script-title=ja:天皇制国家と宗教 |author-link=村上重良}}</ref> so people in Tokyo could worship the deities enshrined at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Grand Shrine of Ise]] from afar. Back then it was originally called ''Hibiya Daijingu''.<ref name=":0" />

In 1901, a wedding took place at the shrine, being the first [[:en:Shinto wedding|Shinto wedding]] held in an urban area.<ref>{{Cite book|last=de-Gaia|first=Susan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=jt91DwAAQBAJ&dq=Tokyo+Daijingu+history&pg=RA1-PA286|title=Encyclopedia of Women in World Religions: Faith and Culture across History [2 volumes]|date=2018-11-16|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-4408-4850-6|language=en}}</ref>{{Rp|286}}

After the [[:en:1923 Great Kantō earthquake|Kanto Earthquake]], the shrine was moved to [[:en:Iidabashi|Iidabashi]] in 1928 and renamed to ''Iidabashi Daijingu''. Then after [[:en:Pacific War|World War 2]], the place changed its name to Tokyo Daijingu.<ref name=":0" />

== Enshrined kami ==
Deities enshrined here include.<ref name=":0" />

* [[Amaterasu]]
* [[:en:Toyouke-hime|Toyouke-no-Ohkami]]
* [[Ame-no-Minakanushi|Ameno-Minakanushi]]
* [[Takamimusubi]]
* [[Kamimusubi]]
* [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]]

'''Tokyo Daijingu''' is a shrine located in [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]].<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|last=Bureau|first=Tokyo Convention & Visitors|title=Tokyo Daijingu Shrine|url=https://www.gotokyo.org/en/spot/42/index.html|access-date=2021-11-07|website=The Official Tokyo Travel Guide, GO TOKYO|language=en}}</ref>  The shrine is also called ''O-Ise-sama in Tokyo'' because of the deities enshrined there.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=English - Tokyo Daijingu|url=http://www.tokyodaijingu.or.jp/english/|access-date=2021-11-07|website=www.tokyodaijingu.or.jp|language=ja}}</ref> It is one of the top five shrines in Tokyo.<ref name=":1" />

== History ==
The shrine was built in the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Hardacre|first=Helen|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=nP7vr5yaaZIC&dq=Tokyo+Daijingu&pg=PA89|title=Shinto and the State, 1868-1988|date=1989|publisher=Princeton University Press|isbn=978-0-691-02052-5|language=en}}</ref>{{Rp|89}} by [[Jingūkyō|Jingu-kyo]]<ref>{{Cite book |last=村上 |first=重良 |title= |date=August 2007 |publisher=講談社 |isbn=978-4061598324 |series=講談社学術文庫 |pages=117–118 |script-title=ja:天皇制国家と宗教 |author-link=村上重良}}</ref> so people in Tokyo could worship the deities enshrined at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Grand Shrine of Ise]] from afar. Back then it was originally called ''Hibiya Daijingu''.<ref name=":0" />

In 1901, a wedding took place at the shrine, being the first [[:en:Shinto wedding|Shinto wedding]] held in an urban area.<ref>{{Cite book|last=de-Gaia|first=Susan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=jt91DwAAQBAJ&dq=Tokyo+Daijingu+history&pg=RA1-PA286|title=Encyclopedia of Women in World Religions: Faith and Culture across History [2 volumes]|date=2018-11-16|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-4408-4850-6|language=en}}</ref>{{Rp|286}}

After the [[:en:1923 Great Kantō earthquake|Kanto Earthquake]], the shrine was moved to [[:en:Iidabashi|Iidabashi]] in 1928 and renamed to ''Iidabashi Daijingu''. Then after [[:en:Pacific War|World War 2]], the place changed its name to Tokyo Daijingu.<ref name=":0" />

== Enshrined kami ==
Deities enshrined here include.<ref name=":0" />

* [[Amaterasu]]
* [[:en:Toyouke-hime|Toyouke-no-Ohkami]]
* [[Ame-no-Minakanushi|Ameno-Minakanushi]]
* [[Takamimusubi]]
* [[Kamimusubi]]
* [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]]

== History ==
The shrine was built in the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Hardacre|first=Helen|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=nP7vr5yaaZIC&dq=Tokyo+Daijingu&pg=PA89|title=Shinto and the State, 1868-1988|date=1989|publisher=Princeton University Press|isbn=978-0-691-02052-5|language=en}}</ref>{{Rp|89}} by [[Jingūkyō|Jingu-kyo]]<ref>{{Cite book |last=村上 |first=重良 |title= |date=August 2007 |publisher=講談社 |isbn=978-4061598324 |series=講談社学術文庫 |pages=117–118 |script-title=ja:天皇制国家と宗教 |author-link=村上重良}}</ref> so people in Tokyo could worship the deities enshrined at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Grand Shrine of Ise]] from afar. Back then it was originally called ''Hibiya Daijingu''.<ref name=":0" />

In 1901, a wedding took place at the shrine, being the first [[:en:Shinto wedding|Shinto wedding]] held in an urban area.<ref>{{Cite book|last=de-Gaia|first=Susan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=jt91DwAAQBAJ&dq=Tokyo+Daijingu+history&pg=RA1-PA286|title=Encyclopedia of Women in World Religions: Faith and Culture across History [2 volumes]|date=2018-11-16|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-4408-4850-6|language=en}}</ref>{{Rp|286}}

After the [[:en:1923 Great Kantō earthquake|Kanto Earthquake]], the shrine was moved to [[:en:Iidabashi|Iidabashi]] in 1928 and renamed to ''Iidabashi Daijingu''. Then after [[:en:Pacific War|World War 2]], the place changed its name to Tokyo Daijingu.<ref name=":0" />

== Enshrined kami ==
Deities enshrined here include.<ref name=":0" />

* [[Amaterasu]]
* [[:en:Toyouke-hime|Toyouke-no-Ohkami]]
* [[Ame-no-Minakanushi|Ameno-Minakanushi]]
* [[Takamimusubi]]
* [[Kamimusubi]]
* [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]]

'''Tokyo Daijingu''' is a shrine located in [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]].<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|last=Bureau|first=Tokyo Convention & Visitors|title=Tokyo Daijingu Shrine|url=https://www.gotokyo.org/en/spot/42/index.html|access-date=2021-11-07|website=The Official Tokyo Travel Guide, GO TOKYO|language=en}}</ref>  The shrine is also called ''O-Ise-sama in Tokyo'' because of the deities enshrined there.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=English - Tokyo Daijingu|url=http://www.tokyodaijingu.or.jp/english/|access-date=2021-11-07|website=www.tokyodaijingu.or.jp|language=ja}}</ref> It is one of the top five shrines in Tokyo.<ref name=":1" />

== History ==
The shrine was built in the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Hardacre|first=Helen|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=nP7vr5yaaZIC&dq=Tokyo+Daijingu&pg=PA89|title=Shinto and the State, 1868-1988|date=1989|publisher=Princeton University Press|isbn=978-0-691-02052-5|language=en}}</ref>{{Rp|89}} by [[Jingūkyō|Jingu-kyo]]<ref>{{Cite book |last=村上 |first=重良 |title= |date=August 2007 |publisher=講談社 |isbn=978-4061598324 |series=講談社学術文庫 |pages=117–118 |script-title=ja:天皇制国家と宗教 |author-link=村上重良}}</ref> so people in Tokyo could worship the deities enshrined at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Grand Shrine of Ise]] from afar. Back then it was originally called ''Hibiya Daijingu''.<ref name=":0" />

In 1901, a wedding took place at the shrine, being the first [[:en:Shinto wedding|Shinto wedding]] held in an urban area.<ref>{{Cite book|last=de-Gaia|first=Susan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=jt91DwAAQBAJ&dq=Tokyo+Daijingu+history&pg=RA1-PA286|title=Encyclopedia of Women in World Religions: Faith and Culture across History [2 volumes]|date=2018-11-16|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-4408-4850-6|language=en}}</ref>{{Rp|286}}

After the [[:en:1923 Great Kantō earthquake|Kanto Earthquake]], the shrine was moved to [[:en:Iidabashi|Iidabashi]] in 1928 and renamed to ''Iidabashi Daijingu''. Then after [[:en:Pacific War|World War 2]], the place changed its name to Tokyo Daijingu.<ref name=":0" />

== Enshrined kami ==
Deities enshrined here include.<ref name=":0" />

* [[Amaterasu]]
* [[:en:Toyouke-hime|Toyouke-no-Ohkami]]
* [[Ame-no-Minakanushi|Ameno-Minakanushi]]
* [[Takamimusubi]]
* [[Kamimusubi]]
* [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]]

In 1901, a wedding took place at the shrine, being the first {{ill|Shinto wedding|en|Shinto wedding|WD=Q18215243|lt=Shinto wedding}} held in an urban area.<ref>{{Cite book|last=de-Gaia|first=Susan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=jt91DwAAQBAJ&dq=Tokyo+Daijingu+history&pg=RA1-PA286|title=Encyclopedia of Women in World Religions: Faith and Culture across History [2 volumes]|date=2018-11-16|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-4408-4850-6|language=en}}</ref>{{Rp|286}}

'''Tokyo Daijingu''' is a shrine located in {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo}}.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|last=Bureau|first=Tokyo Convention & Visitors|title=Tokyo Daijingu Shrine|url=https://www.gotokyo.org/en/spot/42/index.html|access-date=2021-11-07|website=The Official Tokyo Travel Guide, GO TOKYO|language=en}}</ref>  The shrine is also called ''O-Ise-sama in Tokyo'' because of the deities enshrined there.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=English - Tokyo Daijingu|url=http://www.tokyodaijingu.or.jp/english/|access-date=2021-11-07|website=www.tokyodaijingu.or.jp|language=ja}}</ref> It is one of the top five shrines in Tokyo.<ref name=":1" />

== History ==
The shrine was built in the early {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji period}}<ref>{{Cite book|last=Hardacre|first=Helen|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=nP7vr5yaaZIC&dq=Tokyo+Daijingu&pg=PA89|title=Shinto and the State, 1868-1988|date=1989|publisher=Princeton University Press|isbn=978-0-691-02052-5|language=en}}</ref>{{Rp|89}} by [[Jingūkyō|Jingu-kyo]]<ref>{{Cite book |last=村上 |first=重良 |title= |date=August 2007 |publisher=講談社 |isbn=978-4061598324 |series=講談社学術文庫 |pages=117–118 |script-title=ja:天皇制国家と宗教 |author-link=村上重良}}</ref> so people in Tokyo could worship the deities enshrined at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Grand Shrine of Ise]] from afar. Back then it was originally called ''Hibiya Daijingu''.<ref name=":0" />

In 1901, a wedding took place at the shrine, being the first {{ill|Shinto wedding|en|Shinto wedding|WD=Q18215243|lt=Shinto wedding}} held in an urban area.<ref>{{Cite book|last=de-Gaia|first=Susan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=jt91DwAAQBAJ&dq=Tokyo+Daijingu+history&pg=RA1-PA286|title=Encyclopedia of Women in World Religions: Faith and Culture across History [2 volumes]|date=2018-11-16|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-4408-4850-6|language=en}}</ref>{{Rp|286}}

After the [[:en:1923 Great Kantō earthquake|Kanto Earthquake]], the shrine was moved to {{ill|Iidabashi|en|Iidabashi|WD=Q5994914|lt=Iidabashi}} in 1928 and renamed to ''Iidabashi Daijingu''. Then after {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=World War 2}}, the place changed its name to Tokyo Daijingu.<ref name=":0" />

== Enshrined kami ==
Deities enshrined here include.<ref name=":0" />

* [[Amaterasu]]
* {{ill|Toyouke-hime|en|Toyouke-hime|WD=Q2983724|lt=Toyouke-no-Ohkami}}
* [[Ame-no-Minakanushi|Ameno-Minakanushi]]
* [[Takamimusubi]]
* [[Kamimusubi]]
* {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|en|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|WD=Q4388732|lt=Yamatohime-no-mikoto}}

After the [[:en:1923 Great Kantō earthquake|Kanto Earthquake]], the shrine was moved to {{ill|Iidabashi|en|Iidabashi|WD=Q5994914|lt=Iidabashi}} in 1928 and renamed to ''Iidabashi Daijingu''. Then after {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=World War 2}}, the place changed its name to Tokyo Daijingu.<ref name=":0" />

== Enshrined kami ==
Deities enshrined here include.<ref name=":0" />

* [[Amaterasu]]
* {{ill|Toyouke-hime|en|Toyouke-hime|WD=Q2983724|lt=Toyouke-no-Ohkami}}

'''Tokyo Daijingu''' is a shrine located in {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo|1=Tokyo}}.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|last=Bureau|first=Tokyo Convention & Visitors|title=Tokyo Daijingu Shrine|url=https://www.gotokyo.org/en/spot/42/index.html|access-date=2021-11-07|website=The Official Tokyo Travel Guide, GO TOKYO|language=en}}</ref>  The shrine is also called ''O-Ise-sama in Tokyo'' because of the deities enshrined there.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=English - Tokyo Daijingu|url=http://www.tokyodaijingu.or.jp/english/|access-date=2021-11-07|website=www.tokyodaijingu.or.jp|language=ja}}</ref> It is one of the top five shrines in Tokyo.<ref name=":1" />

== History ==
The shrine was built in the early {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era}}<ref>{{Cite book|last=Hardacre|first=Helen|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=nP7vr5yaaZIC&dq=Tokyo+Daijingu&pg=PA89|title=Shinto and the State, 1868-1988|date=1989|publisher=Princeton University Press|isbn=978-0-691-02052-5|language=en}}</ref>{{Rp|89}} by [[Jingūkyō|Jingu-kyo]]<ref>{{Cite book |last=村上 |first=重良 |title= |date=August 2007 |publisher=講談社 |isbn=978-4061598324 |series=講談社学術文庫 |pages=117–118 |script-title=ja:天皇制国家と宗教 |author-link=村上重良}}</ref> so people in Tokyo could worship the deities enshrined at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Grand Shrine of Ise]] from afar. Back then it was originally called ''Hibiya Daijingu''.<ref name=":0" />

In 1901, a wedding took place at the shrine, being the first {{ill|Shinto wedding|en|Shinto wedding|WD=Q18215243|lt=Shinto wedding|1=Shinto wedding}} held in an urban area.<ref>{{Cite book|last=de-Gaia|first=Susan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=jt91DwAAQBAJ&dq=Tokyo+Daijingu+history&pg=RA1-PA286|title=Encyclopedia of Women in World Religions: Faith and Culture across History [2 volumes]|date=2018-11-16|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-4408-4850-6|language=en}}</ref>{{Rp|286}}

After the [[:en:1923 Great Kantō earthquake|Kanto Earthquake]], the shrine was moved to {{ill|Iidabashi|en|Iidabashi|WD=Q5994914|lt=Iidabashi|1=Iidabashi}} in 1928 and renamed to ''Iidabashi Daijingu''. Then after {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=World War 2|1=Pacific War}}, the place changed its name to Tokyo Daijingu.<ref name=":0" />

== Enshrined kami ==
Deities enshrined here include.<ref name=":0" />

* [[Amaterasu]]
* {{ill|Toyouke-hime|en|Toyouke-hime|WD=Q2983724|lt=Toyouke-no-Ohkami|1=Toyouke-hime}}
* [[Ame-no-Minakanushi|Ameno-Minakanushi]]
* [[Takamimusubi]]

{{Infobox religious building
| name                  = Tokyo Daijingu
| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]
| image                 = File:Tokyo Daijingu 2019e.jpg
| location              = Address : 2-4-1, Fujimi, Chiyoda-ku, Tokyo, 102-0071 Japan
| deity                 = {{ill|Amaterasu|WD=Q455602|1=Amaterasu}}; {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|WD=Q2983724|1=Toyouke-hime}}
| website               = http://www.tokyodaijingu.or.jp/index.html
| prefecture= {{ill|Fujimi|WD=Q3347253|1=Fujimi, Chiyoda, Tokyo}}
| coordinates= {{Coord|35.69998333|N|139.74686944|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}
}}

'''Tokyo Daijingu''' is a shrine located in {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo|1=Tokyo}}.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|last=Bureau|first=Tokyo Convention & Visitors|title=Tokyo Daijingu Shrine|url=https://www.gotokyo.org/en/spot/42/index.html|access-date=2021-11-07|website=The Official Tokyo Travel Guide, GO TOKYO|language=en}}</ref>  The shrine is also called ''O-Ise-sama in Tokyo'' because of the deities enshrined there.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=English - Tokyo Daijingu|url=http://www.tokyodaijingu.or.jp/english/|access-date=2021-11-07|website=www.tokyodaijingu.or.jp|language=ja}}</ref> It is one of the top five shrines in Tokyo.<ref name=":1" />

== History ==
The shrine was built in the early {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era}}<ref>{{Cite book|last=Hardacre|first=Helen|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=nP7vr5yaaZIC&dq=Tokyo+Daijingu&pg=PA89|title=Shinto and the State, 1868-1988|date=1989|publisher=Princeton University Press|isbn=978-0-691-02052-5|language=en}}</ref>{{Rp|89}} by [[Jingūkyō|Jingu-kyo]]<ref>{{Cite book |last=村上 |first=重良 |title= |date=August 2007 |publisher=講談社 |isbn=978-4061598324 |series=講談社学術文庫 |pages=117–118 |script-title=ja:天皇制国家と宗教 |author-link=村上重良}}</ref> so people in Tokyo could worship the deities enshrined at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Grand Shrine of Ise]] from afar. Back then it was originally called ''Hibiya Daijingu''.<ref name=":0" />

In 1901, a wedding took place at the shrine, being the first {{ill|Shinto wedding|en|Shinto wedding|WD=Q18215243|lt=Shinto wedding|1=Shinto wedding}} held in an urban area.<ref>{{Cite book|last=de-Gaia|first=Susan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=jt91DwAAQBAJ&dq=Tokyo+Daijingu+history&pg=RA1-PA286|title=Encyclopedia of Women in World Religions: Faith and Culture across History [2 volumes]|date=2018-11-16|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-4408-4850-6|language=en}}</ref>{{Rp|286}}

After the [[:en:1923 Great Kantō earthquake|Kanto Earthquake]], the shrine was moved to {{ill|Iidabashi|en|Iidabashi|WD=Q5994914|lt=Iidabashi|1=Iidabashi}} in 1928 and renamed to ''Iidabashi Daijingu''. Then after {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=World War 2|1=Pacific War}}, the place changed its name to Tokyo Daijingu.<ref name=":0" />

== Enshrined kami ==
Deities enshrined here include.<ref name=":0" />

* [[Amaterasu]]
* {{ill|Toyouke-hime|en|Toyouke-hime|WD=Q2983724|lt=Toyouke-no-Ohkami|1=Toyouke-hime}}
* [[Ame-no-Minakanushi|Ameno-Minakanushi]]
* [[Takamimusubi]]
* [[Kamimusubi]]
* {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|en|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|WD=Q4388732|lt=Yamatohime-no-mikoto|1=Yamatohime-no-mikoto}}
